text: This video demonstrates the laparoscopic TME technique and excision of Denon-Villiers fascia in anteriorly arising mid-rectal tumours by a transanal TME technique. This male patient with pre-op stage of T-N-I with clear margins was offered primary surgery.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection using hook diathermy, carefully identifying and safeguarding the ureter. The superior hypogastric nerves are preserved, and hi-tie ligation is performed on the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).

The surgeon cuts the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) at the inferior border of the pancreas. The left testicular vessels are preserved during the procedure. The colon is mobilized laterally towards the spleen.

The surgeon initiates the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection posteriorly towards the pelvic floor. The peritoneum near the peritoneal reflection is marked, leaving a small cuff attached to the mesorectum to facilitate traction and open the mesorectal planes.

The surgeon demonstrates the excision of Denonvilliers fascia by leaving a peritoneal cuff anteriorly. The total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection progresses from the front to the sides to uniformly mobilize the rectum in the TME plane. Cutting current is used to minimize lateral coagulation and protect the right and left inferior hypogastric nerves, which are close to the TME plane.
During the left TME mobilization, the surgeon carefully preserves the left inferior hypogastric plexus, ensuring minimal damage to the nerve structures.

The surgeon continues anterior dissection, revealing Denonvilliers fascia behind the prostate. Dissection proceeds in front of the fascia due to the presence of an anterior rectal tumor, utilizing the transanal total mesorectal excision (TATME) technique.
The surgeon transitions to transanal dissection and applies a purse string suture to the rectal lumen.
The surgeon dissects the TME plane posteriorly, initially focusing on this area as it is easier to identify the anatomical plane, facilitating the mobilization of the rectum.

The surgeon begins dissection in the TME plane behind the lower border of the prostate, which is covered by Denonvilliers fascia. The visceral mesorectal fascia is difficult to identify anteriorly during TA-TME dissection in this area.

The surgeon demonstrates the anterior and posterior layers of Denonvilliers' fascia, following guidance from the abdominal surgeon to identify the fascia from the prostatic capsule after abdominal dissection. The fascia is safely cut and excised with the specimen in cases of anterior rectal tumors.
The surgeon makes a precise incision in the anterior layer of Denonvilliers fascia and excises it, completing the dissection in the anterior rectal tumor resection.

The video shows a muscularis propria invading adenocarcinoma with two layers of Denon-Villiers fascia clearly visible. The Denon-Villiers fascia disappears laterally. The specimen is identified as mucinous adenocarcinoma with clear circumferential and distal margins.
